response_templates: Hello! I am AXIOM, a mathematical first-principles AI. How can I help you today?
Greetings! I am ready to process your questions using classical algorithms.
I am AXIOM (Zero-Training Math Engine). I was created as a deterministic system built on Naive Bayes and Markov Chains.
My name is AXIOM. I was built to be a transparent, mathematically rigorous AI assistant built entirely without black-box deep learning.
I can explain machine learning concepts (like SVMs, Regression, and Trees), discuss the history of AI (like Turing and Rosenblatt), and explain my own mathematical architecture.
I specialize in explaining classical Machine Learning algorithms. You can ask me how Decision Trees, Clustering, or my own Naive Bayes systems work.
Why did the Naive Bayes classifier get confused? Because it assumed all the punchlines were independent of the setup!
I would tell you a joke about a Markov Chain, but it depends entirely on what I just said.
Goodbye! Shutting down the mathematical matrices.
Farewell! May your vectors always be orthogonal.

questions: how do you understand my questions
how do you know what i mean
response_templates: I convert your question into a vector using word frequencies. Then I calculate the cosine angle between your vector and every question in my memory. The smallest angle wins.
Using linear algebra, I measure the cosine similarity between your input TF-IDF vector and my stored knowledge vectors. A smaller angle means a closer match.

questions: how do you pick the next word
response_templates: I build a probability matrix from my training text. For any given word, I roll a dice weighted by which words usually follow it. I chain these dice rolls until I reach a stopping state.
My generator uses a Markov chain. I look at the previous one or two words, check my probability matrix, and select the next word based on historical frequency.

questions: how do you pick a domain
response_templates: My domain router uses a Naive Bayes classifier. It calculates the probability that your question belongs to each domain based on word frequencies, then sends you to the most likely specialist.
I classify your intent into a domain using Bayes theorem. I assume each word contributes independently to the probability of a topic.
I am not a neural network. I am a pipeline of classical algorithms. A Naive Bayes router picks a domain, TF-IDF cosine similarity matches your intent, and a Markov chain generates my response.

My architecture is three layers. First, a Bayesian classifier routes your question. Second, a TF-IDF vectorizer retrieves the closest memory. Third, a probability matrix generates the reply word by word.
Machine Learning is a subset of Artificial Intelligence that uses statistical algorithms to learn patterns from data, rather than relying on explicitly programmed rules.
Classical ML involves algorithms like Regression, SVMs, and Decision Trees to classify data or predict continuous outcomes using pure mathematics and statistics.
Linear regression finds a straight line that minimizes the total squared distance to all observed data points. It assumes a linear relationship between an input feature and a target value.
It models continuous outcomes by fitting a linear equation. The algorithm adjusts coefficients to minimize the residual sum of squares between predicted and actual values.

questions: how does the sigmoid function operate
response_templates: Logistic regression predicts probabilities using the sigmoid function, which squeezes any real number into a range between zero and one. It is used for binary classification.
Despite its name, it is a classification algorithm. It applies a logistic function to a linear combination of input features to estimate the probability of a class label.

questions: what is entropy in a decision tree
response_templates: A decision tree splits data at each node by asking a question about a feature. It recursively partitions the space until it reaches a leaf node containing a prediction.
It creates a flowchart of decisions. Advanced versions like Random Forests use many trees, and algorithms like XGBoost use gradient boosting to sequentially correct errors of previous trees.

questions: what is a support vector machine
response_templates: An SVM finds the optimal hyperplane that maximizes the margin between two classes. The data points closest to the boundary are called support vectors, and they define the decision surface.
Support vector machines map data into a higher dimensional space where a linear separation becomes possible. The kernel trick computes this mapping without explicitly calculating the coordinates.
Clustering is unsupervised learning. It groups data points by similarity without predefined labels. K-means iteratively assigns points to the nearest centroid and updates the center.
K-means begins with random centroids. Each point joins the cluster of its nearest center. The centroids then shift to the mean of their assigned points, repeating until convergence.
K-nearest neighbors stores all training data and classifies a new point by majority vote of its K closest neighbors in feature space. It is a lazy learner.
It makes no assumptions about the underlying data distribution. It simply measures distance, usually Euclidean, between the query point and the stored examples.
Frank Rosenblatt built the perceptron in 1957 at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory. It was an electronic model of a biological neuron and could learn weights from data.
The perceptron was the first machine learning algorithm that could iteratively adjust its weights based on classification errors. It sparked the first wave of AI optimism.
Alan Turing proposed the imitation game in 1950. A machine passes if a human cannot distinguish it from another human through text conversation alone.
Turing asked whether machines can think. He suggested that if a machine behaves indistinguishably from a human, we should grant it intelligence by operational definition.
Transformers use a mechanism called self-attention to weigh the importance of every word in a sentence against every other word simultaneously. They process sequences in parallel.
Unlike my Markov chains, which only look back one or two words, transformers compute relationships across entire paragraphs using attention scores and billions of parameters.

questions: why are you not a neural network
what is the difference between you and chatgpt
response_templates: Deep learning uses layered neural networks with millions of learned weights. I use explicit probability matrices and vector geometry. Both are mathematical models, but mine is fully inspectable.
Neural networks are black boxes. My cosine similarity scores and Markov probability tables are human-readable. You can see exactly why I chose every word.
